nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, the Black Back Butterflyfish occupies coral-rich reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs, typically at depths of 2 to 20 metres.

diet.notes: In the wild this species feeds primarily on octocorallian and scleractinian coral polyps. In captivity it has been reported to accept a varied meaty diet including mysis shrimp, krill, chopped marine fish flesh, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and frozen carnivore preparations. Feeding several small meals throughout the day is generally advised, especially for newly introduced specimens. Vitamin and color-enhancing supplements are commonly recommended to help maintain coloration. Some individuals have been reported to eat Aiptasia, though this should not be relied upon.

avoidWith: conspecifics unless introduced as a mated pair

imageCandidates.notes: Leonard Low; 2192x1672. Tack-sharp side profile, whole fish, bright reef background; clearly shows yellow body/fins, diagonal black stripes, dark dorsal back patch and eye bar.
Rickard Zerpe; 2660x1995. Striking dark-background portrait, vibrant yellow fins pop, sharp; full diagnostic pattern visible.
Rickard Zerpe; 2447x1835. Sharp whole-fish shot over natural reef, different angle/background for variety.

summary: The Black Back Butterflyfish (Chaetodon melannotus) is an Indo-Pacific species with a yellow body crossed by diagonal black stripes, generally considered moderately challenging and best kept in a mature fish-only with live rock system due to its wild diet of coral polyps.
careNotes:
Chaetodon melannotus is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa east to Samoa, and from southern Japan south to Lord Howe Island. It is typically encountered as solitary individuals or in pairs across coral-rich reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs.

It is generally considered not reef-safe. In the wild it has been documented feeding on both soft (octocorallian) and stony (scleractinian) coral polyps, and similar nipping behavior is commonly reported in captivity. For this reason it is generally advised to keep this species in a fish-only with live rock (FOWLR) system rather than a mixed reef.

This butterflyfish is generally peaceful with dissimilar tankmates but tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and other similar-looking butterflyfishes. It has been documented to form lifelong monogamous pair bonds; an established mated pair can usually be kept together but single specimens should generally not be combined later. Captive breeding has not been reliably achieved due to a pelagic larval phase.

New specimens are generally reported to adapt readily to captivity and to accept prepared foods, and the species is commonly considered relatively straightforward to feed once settled. A varied meaty diet with vitamin supplementation is commonly advised to support long-term coloration and health.
breedingNotes: The Black Back Butterflyfish has been reported to form monogamous pairs that spawn pelagically in the wild, releasing eggs into open water where larvae drift as plankton. Captive breeding has not been reliably achieved, as the pelagic larval phase typical of the family Chaetodontidae is extremely difficult to replicate in home aquariums.

fish.breedingNotes: A pelagic spawner that releases planktonic larvae into open water. Captive breeding has not been reliably achieved in home aquariums.
